lines.text: The doors of Ridgeview Academy loom ahead — glass and brick, and a banner reading WELCOME BACK, FALCONS in letters starting to peel at the corners.
Okay. Cool. Normal building. Normal doors. You've done doors before.
Rowan's fingers find a rhythm on her thigh — tap tap-tap, tap tap-tap — before she notices and shoves her hand into her pocket instead.
lines.action: taps out a rhythm, catches herself

lines.text: Room 214. Room 214. It's not going to change if I stare at it a fourth time.
She takes a breath meant to be steadying. Mostly it just makes her aware of how loud breathing sounds when you're paying attention to it.
New school number four. You'd think there'd be a system by now.
There is not a system.
Homeroom 214 is a low hum of backpacks hitting floors and half-finished conversations.
Alright, folks, eyes up here —
lines.action: does a slow scan of the room

lines.text: well, most of you. Priya, sketchbook can survive thirty more seconds, I promise.
Priya looks up just long enough to close it — not annoyed, exactly. Just closed.
We've got a new face today. Rowan Mercado, that's you, right? C'mon up. Give us something real.
lines.action: taps two fingers on the desk

lines.text: Twenty-some heads turn. In the back row, a boy is already grinning like he knows something nobody else does — Jordan, according to the seating chart taped by the door — leaning forward on his elbows.
Okay. Okay okay okay. Just — say words. In an order. That make sense.
Hi — I'm Rowan. I just moved here from Portland, but don't worry, I'm not gonna make you sit through the whole saga.
lines.action: stands, decides to just go for it

lines.text: A couple kids laugh — real ones, not pity ones.
This is move number four, so I've gotten weirdly good at the new-kid thing. Not, like, good good. Just — experienced.
Respect. Four schools, zero fear.
Rowan Mercado. I like that. Sit down before I make you say more.
Rowan sits, pulse still going — but it's a good kind of loud, for once.
Okay. That didn't completely fall apart. Noted.
Um — hi. I'm Rowan. I just moved here. That's — yeah. That's it.
She's sitting before Ms. Alvarez even nods, cheeks warm, staring hard at the scratched-up desk like it might have answers written on it somewhere.
Rowan Mercado. Welcome to 214.
Across the room, Priya glances up from her sketchbook — just a flicker, there and gone — like something about that registered.
That's it. That's all anyone's getting today.
Hi, I'm Rowan. Fair warning, I already got lost twice this morning, so if you see me walk into a supply closet with real confidence, just — let me have that.
The class laughs — actual, unforced. Jordan laughs loudest, slapping the desk.
Bro got lost TWICE? On day one?? Okay, I respect the commitment to the bit.
In the corner, Priya smiles down at her sketchbook — small, but real.
Noted for the record. Sit, before the supply closet becomes a running theme.
Okay. That worked. Don't get used to it.
The cafeteria hits like a wall of sound — trays clattering, a hundred conversations stacked on top of each other.
Yo! Rowan-dawg! Over here, c'mon!
lines.action: waving big from a packed table

lines.text: Across the room, by the window, Priya sits alone, headphones in, pen tapping against her teeth, sketchbook open in front of her like a shield.
Okay but like. Loud table or quiet corner. Big decisions before I've even eaten.
Rowan slides her tray onto the table. Four kids look up, size her up, go back to their conversation like she passed some invisible test.
See, told you. This is basically the law now. New kid sits with us, day one.
Okay so hot take — Coach Delgado's whistle count today was personally aggressive. Blew it four times just walking past me.
The table laughs. Someone mimics the whistle. It's fast and funny — and one joke lands a little sharp, aimed at some kid two tables over. Nobody else seems to notice.
Anyway. You're funny, Rowan-dawg. I like it. You're in.
In. Okay. That was easy. Weirdly easy.
Hey — this seat taken?
...No. Go for it.
A beat of quiet that isn't quite comfortable yet.
Okay but like, is that a dragon? I feel like I need context.
It's not a dragon. Dragons are lazy worldbuilding.
She hesitates — then turns the sketchbook, just slightly.
This is a Kessu. They live in flooded cities. Only come out at low tide, 'cause that's when the light does this thing—
—it doesn't matter.
No, it matters. Keep going.
Priya looks up — actually looks — like she's checking whether that was real.
...Yeah? Okay.
lines.action: the pen starts moving again, looser this time

lines.text: So this one's not finished — the linework's off around the gills, but—
Okay. This is nice. This is actually nice.
Rowan picks the emptiest table in the corner, tray down, like this was always the plan.
Just observe today. That's not sad. That's strategic.
It's quieter here. Quiet enough to hear herself chew, which is its own kind of information.
Tomorrow. Tomorrow I'll figure out where I go. Today's just recon.
Across the room, laughter rises from the loud table. Rowan doesn't look over. Much.
Alright, folks — group project, and before anyone starts negotiating: no, you don't get to pick your partner.
lines.action: taps two fingers on the desk

lines.text: A groan ripples through the room.
Partners aren't punishment, Jordan, despite your face right now.
Rowan, you're with Priya. Priya, be nice — she's new, she doesn't know your villain-origin-story reputation yet.
Priya's mouth twitches — almost a smile — before she catches herself and goes still again.
...I don't have a reputation. I have a system.
Okay. Let's see how this goes.
Okay so I'm thinking we just do the ecosystem diagram thing, everyone always does it, it's simple, it's safe, we just—
...Sure.
Her sketchbook stays closed on the desk, both hands resting on top of it — a habit, not a choice.
You cool with that?
Yeah. It's fine.
It sounds fine. It doesn't quite sound true.
That was fast. Almost too fast.
Okay but like — before I decide anything, what were you thinking? You had that look.
...What look.
The pen-tapping look. That's a thinking-face. I've seen it.
Priya's mouth does the almost-smile thing again — but this time it finishes.
Okay, so — what if instead of the boring diagram, we made it a story? Like, the ecosystem has a whole narrative. Characters. I could draw it.
Wait, that's actually really good.
...Yeah? Okay. Yeah, I'd like that.
She opens the sketchbook fully this time, and turns it so they can both see.
Whatever you think is best, honestly. I don't really have opinions on this stuff.
...Okay. I guess we could do the diagram. Or — I don't know. Whatever's easiest.
lines.emotion: expecting more pushback that never comes

lines.text: She makes the decisions, one after another, and Rowan nods along to each one.
Kind of doing this alone either way, huh.
What?
Nothing. Diagram it is.
That wasn't nothing. Say something. ...Say something.
The hallway between classes, crowd thickening near the lockers. Jordan's got Priya's sketchbook raised over his head, flipping pages one-handed.
'The Kessu weep saltwater when the tide turns—' bro, what IS this, are we in third grade—
lines.emotion: performing, voice pitched high and mocking

lines.text: A ring of kids laughs. Priya's face goes red, then very still — the flat, quiet kind of hurt that doesn't look like hurt to people who aren't paying attention.
Give it back.
Nah wait, there's a whole page of names, she's got, like, LORE—
lines.emotion: not hearing her, or not caring

lines.text: Every face turns toward Rowan. Waiting to see which way this goes.
Okay. Okay. Say something. Or don't. Everyone's watching either way.
Jordan. Give it back.
The crowd's laughter stutters, dies.
...What? It's just a joke, everybody thought it was funny.
It's hers. Give it back.
A beat. Jordan looks around — for backup, for the joke to land again — and finds neither. He hands the sketchbook back, quieter now, younger-sounding.
Wait, you're actually mad at me right now?
Yeah. Kind of.
Priya takes the sketchbook back with both hands, holding it against her chest. She looks at Rowan like she's redoing the math on something.
...Thanks.
The crowd's already dissolving. Jordan lingers a second too long before walking off, fidgeting.
Rowan laughs — a beat too late, a little too loud, but a laugh.
Right?? Bro, the NAMES she gives these things—
Priya's eyes flick to Rowan. Just once. Something in her face closes — quiet and complete, like a door that doesn't slam, just clicks shut.
Can I have it back now.
lines.emotion: flat, to no one in particular

lines.text: Jordan tosses it to her, already bored, already onto the next thing. Priya walks off without looking at anyone.
That's fine. That's — that's fine. Everyone laughed. It's fine.
Okay but like, if we're roasting hobbies, Jordan, we need to talk about your fantasy football team name. 'QB Steve Buscemi'? Explain.
lines.action: cutting in before Jordan can read more

lines.text: The crowd's attention swivels, laughing, already forgetting the sketchbook. Jordan, delighted despite himself, lowers it.
Yo that team is UNDEFEATED, don't play me—
In the confusion, Priya slips the sketchbook out of Jordan's loose grip and disappears down the hall, fast, without a word.
Okay. That worked. I think. I don't actually know if that worked.
The library's mostly empty, late-afternoon light doing something gold across the table. Priya's got the presentation slides open, and her sketchbook — closed — right beside them.
So I had this idea. For the presentation. But it's probably too much.
What idea?
The illustrated story thing. Like, actually drawn out, not just a slide with bullet points. But nobody else is doing it like that, so.
lines.action: opens the sketchbook, slides it over, tentative

lines.text: It's fine if it's dumb. I have a backup slide either way.
lines.action: taps her pen against her teeth

lines.text: Okay. What do I actually say here.
It's not dumb. It's the best idea in this whole assignment. We should do it your way.
Priya looks up, startled — like she hadn't actually expected a yes.
...Yeah? Okay.
Okay. Let's do it your— I mean. Our way.
lines.emotion: small smile, the first unguarded one today

lines.text: She opens the sketchbook fully, no hesitation this time, and starts sketching the layout right there on the table.
There it is. That's the smile from homeroom. Good.
Okay but like — maybe we just do it the way the Delgado-Martinez group's doing it? Slides, bullet points, safe. Less risky.
Priya's quiet for a second too long.
...Yeah. Sure. That's probably smarter.
She closes the sketchbook — fully, not just flipping it shut — and slides it to the edge of the table, out of the way.
I'll redo the slides tonight.
That was the right call. Probably. It was fine. It was — fine.
Alright, folks, let's hear it. Rowan, Priya — you're up.
They present. It goes the way it goes — better or worse depending on what got decided in the library, but it's theirs, for better or worse.
From his seat, Jordan watches quieter than usual — more interested in Priya's face than the slides.
That's a real detail, whichever way you went with this. I like real details.
Alright, folks — five minutes, then you're free.
The bell's going to ring soon. Rowan can feel today ending, the shape of it already mostly set.
One day down. However many left to figure out who I actually want to be here.
The steps outside are golden with late light, everyone flooding out around them.
Hey — wait up.
...Oh. Hey.
Okay but like, are you doing anything for lunch tomorrow? Asking for, um. Reasons.
...Yeah. Okay. Yeah, I'd like that.
It's small. Not cinematic. Just two people deciding to keep going.
Jordan's crew spills out the doors laughing, already loud, already planning tomorrow like it's a foregone conclusion.
Yo, Rowan-dawg. Same table tomorrow, obviously.
Obviously.
It feels good. It also feels, a little, like a costume she put on this morning and forgot to take off.
The steps are emptying out, voices fading into the parking lot, into cars, into tomorrow.
Nobody waved. That's fine. Nobody waves on day one.
Rowan pulls out her schedule one more time, even though she has it memorized by now.
Tomorrow. Tomorrow will be different. Probably.

options.text: Hi — I'm Rowan. I just moved here, this is move number four, ask me anything.
Um, hi, I'm Rowan. That's... that's it.
Hi, I'm Rowan, and I already got lost twice today, so, low bar from here.

a.text: Okay. Just go. Loud and real beats quiet and safe. Probably.

b.text: Fewer words, fewer ways to mess this up.

c.text: Joke. Say the joke. Nervous system's already picked one.

options.text: Yeah, I'm coming — save me a seat.
Actually — I think I'll sit over there.
I'm good just chilling here today.

b.text: She's not bothering anyone. I could just... not bother her either.

options.text: Okay, here's what I'm thinking — let's just do the diagram thing.
Wait, what were you picturing? I want to hear it first.
Whatever you think is best. You lead.

a.text: Fast and simple. Get it done, move on.

b.text: She's clearly got something in her head. Might as well ask.

c.text: Safer to agree than to be wrong out loud.

options.text: Okay but like, we need to talk about YOUR hobbies, Jordan.

b.text: Laugh now, feel bad later. Standard plan.

c.text: Don't fight it head-on. Just move it somewhere else.

options.text: Do the real thing. Your way. It's better.
Maybe just copy what the other groups are doing. Safer.

a.text: It's actually good. Why would we do the boring version.

b.text: Nobody ever got in trouble for being exactly like everyone else.

options.text: Yeah, I'm coming — same table tomorrow?

a.text: Say it before you talk yourself out of it.

b.text: This is easy. Easy's good, right?

c.text: Nobody's waiting. That's fine. Keep walking.

lines.text: You know, you're actually the first person today who asked me a question and waited for the answer.
Okay but like, that's a really low bar, Priya.
I know.
lines.action: the kind of laugh that surprises even her

lines.text: That's kind of the problem.
I don't actually know if I said two words to you all day. That's kind of messed up, if I'm honest.
...You don't have to feel bad about it.
I want to anyway. Lunch tomorrow?
...Yeah. Okay.
I spent basically all day trying not to mess anything up. That was not a great plan, it turns out.
lines.emotion: quieter than she means to be

lines.text: Yeah, I noticed you kind of orbiting the room.
Lunch tomorrow? Actual words this time, I promise.
...Yeah. Okay. Yeah, I'd like that.
Bro, today was crazy. You already feel like one of us, not even lying.
...Yeah. I guess I do.
lines.emotion: a beat too long before answering

lines.text: Somewhere behind them, across the yard, a sketchbook closes. Rowan doesn't turn around to check.
See, told you — new kid, day one, already one of the crew.
...Yeah. I guess.
lines.action: glances back toward where Priya usually sits

lines.text: She's probably already gone. That's fine. Tomorrow, maybe.
You coming or what?
Yeah. Coming.
Didn't peg you as a joiner, honestly. Glad you're in, though.
Yeah. Me neither.
Easier to be invisible in a crowd than invisible alone, I guess.
See you tomorrow, same table.
...Yeah. See you.
I sat alone at lunch. I let Priya make every call on the project. I laughed along in the hallway so nobody would look at me twice.
Mission accomplished, I guess. Nobody looked twice.
Priya probably would've said yes. I didn't even ask.
Funny how you can do everything right all day and still walk out the door alone because you didn't say one more sentence.
Jordan's crowd is already three blocks gone, laughing at something that wasn't about me.
Turns out being everyone's favorite for one day doesn't mean anyone waits for you at the door.

voiceNotes: Rowan (player) leans on 'okay but like' as a pivot when she's nervous or trying to redirect a moment toward humor — it's armor as much as it is a verbal tic. Her internal voice runs faster and more anxious than her spoken lines; the gap between what she thinks and what she says is where most of the episode's tension lives. Jordan's dialogue is almost entirely performance — rehearsed-sounding punchlines, nicknames deployed like a handshake — until he's caught off guard (dp-3, dp-6), where the register drops entirely: shorter words, no jokes, genuinely rattled. Priya defaults to clipped, guarded answers with strangers and only opens up through specific, technical detail about her art (never vague compliments-fishing) — her comfort is legible through information density, not warmth of tone. Ms. Alvarez's dry humor lands a beat late for some students by design; she repeats names back warmly ('Rowan Mercado. I like that.') as a small, deliberate act of being seen. Branch dialogue differentiates the shared ending scenes by making each character's exact words reference specific choices made earlier in the day — genuine-path Priya references being asked and waited-for; popularity-path Rowan has to reckon with silence toward Priya; self-protective-path Rowan admits to 'orbiting the room.' None of the endings resolve neatly — that's intentional. First days don't.

alternativeLines.reasoning: Slightly softer or firmer variants depending on how confrontational the studio wants the 'defend' choice to read — current line keeps it clipped and calm, which reads as more mature/controlled courage.

alternatives: That's actually really cool, keep going.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Current line ('No, it matters. Keep going.') is quieter and more sincere; alternatives are slightly more enthusiastic if the scene needs more energy at this beat.

alternatives: So, um. Lunch tomorrow. You in?
Okay but like, we should sit together tomorrow. If you want.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Original keeps Rowan's verbal tic ('okay but like') and trailing hesitance intact for voice consistency; alternatives offer slightly more direct phrasing if a punchier ending beat is preferred.

alternatives: 'They only come out at low tide'— what even IS this—
Bro she's got a whole DICTIONARY for this thing—
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps Jordan's mockery specific and page-referencing rather than generically cruel, which fits his 'quick with a joke, doesn't realize the cost' characterization better than blanket meanness.
